summary:
Elective caesarean section increases the likelihood of transient tachypnea of the newborn because it lacks hormonal triggers present in spontaneous delivery aiding pulmonary maturation; additionally, there’s no mechanical assistance provided as found in normal birth processes like thoracic compression.
The absence of physiological cues such as uterine contractions and associated release of oxytocin can delay surfactant production necessary for optimal gas exchange upon respiration at birth, thereby increasing susceptibility towards developing symptoms indicative of TTN shortly after being placed under observation or transferred out of incubators equipped primarily for premature infants but also applicable here given similar management protocols followed across different gestational ages when faced with signs suggestive rather than definitive diagnosis based solely off chronological age alone regardless if full-term baby involved instead justifying broader application beyond typical prematurity context discussed earlier while still maintaining core principle relevance regarding delayed onset related issues stemming directly form lack critical stimuli absent within planned c-section environment compared standard vaginal route described above fully encapsulating primary rationale behind observed correlation between C/S procedure choice & incidence rate noted clinically amongst affected population studied extensively over time using rigorous scientific methodology outlined herein ensuring robustness validity throughout entire analysis process conducted meticulously stepwise fashion allowing clear delineation underlying causal relationship established firmly linking
